text: part of the earth’. The third, Further Observation on the Terrestrial Convulsions (April 1756), is a refutation of superstitious causes such as invidious astrological conjunctions. Against the theological speculation that the event naturally Liminality and the Modern 98 provoked, Kant insisted on secular explanations. Earthquakes are terrible, but they are accidents, not God’s punishment. As further evidence of how this period was a turning point for Kant, we simply can refer to the three events mentioned in the standard Kant chronology that took place between February and April, between the publication of his second and third essays on the earthquake. In March Kant submitted to the philosophy faculty his third Latin disputation, The Employment in Natural Philosophy of Metaphysics. On 8 April Kant applied for his first position as Professor. On 10 April the public defence of his Physical Monadology was celebrated; the first part of his third essay on the earthquake appeared that very same day. It is therefore not too much to say that some of Kant’s most decisive ideas – that would become foundational for the modern episteme – developed in conjunction with his reflections on this one particular liminal event. While the seismological quality of Kant’s observations remains a matter of debate for specialists, his evolutionary theory of the universe is still today seen as ‘the essence of modern models’ in cosmology (Coles as quoted in Schönfeld 2012). For Kant the question was: how can we recuperate trust in progress and science, and human cognition, in the face of this cataclysm? The Lisbon event inserted itself into a quite crucial debate running through the eighteenth century, concerning the divinity of the cosmos, and the role of good and evil in the world as we know it. In Theodicy, Leibniz had famously defined the universe as ‘the best of all possible worlds’. While nobody denied the existence of evil (as would fatally happen only in the twentieth century), evil was brought under the protective umbrella of a ‘greater good’. Things happened for a reason, and God’s will was good. Voltaire had initially aligned himself with Leibniz and the theological optimists, but the Lisbon earthquake completely broke his faith. Kant wanted to engage with the debates, and give them a positive answer. He needed to domesticate this apparently incomprehensible rupture and total destruction, of which the Lisbon earthquake was exemplary, in order to neutralize the threat such external events posed to the myth of progress grounded in human nature. The answer was given at two levels: first, by searching for natural laws behind the natural event. We see here a stern belief in technology’s promise to liberate man from the destructive power of nature. 1 Second, by reposing and re-founding 1 Kant (1756) forewords his first essay on the Earthquake thus: ‘Man, who is intrusted with the economy [Haushaltung] of the earth, [not only] possesses a capacity, [but] also takes pleasure in learning to know it, and through his insights praises the Creator. Even the terrible instruments of the visitation of the human species, the shakings of countries, the raging of the ocean that is [violently] agitated to its [very] bottom, the fire-spewing mountains, summon men to the contemplation of [nature], and are not less implanted in nature by God as a just consequence of constant laws than other usual causes of incommodity, which are held to be more natural only because we are better acquainted with them’. (Kant 2013, online version) Dimensions of Liminality 99 the question of human knowledge in the world. He would come to ‘cognitively manage’ the threat posed by the Lisbon earthquake in The Analytic of the Sublime of his 1790 Critique of Judgment. Kant never mentions the Lisbon earthquake in the Critique of Judgment, but it is there beneath the entire argument, as a ‘remembered anxiety’ (Ray 2004: 10). These influences on Kant’s thinking point with the utmost clarity towards the crucial links that exist between events, experience and thought in critical, liminal junctures. It is no minor point: it is often asserted that modern philosophy begins with Kant. Modernity is, also in this concrete sense, an institutionalized response to liminal experiences. As a natural event the Lisbon earthquake was a unique phenomenon, unprecedented and unrepeatable; as a large-scale liminal moment it had a widespread effect through the manifold reflections it provoked. In this cognitive and meaning-forming elaboration, it was transformed from a brutal fact to a complex cultural sign, redirecting material and mental processes in Europe and merging with the complex contemporary scientific and political developments that secured its long-term relevance. Kant’s elaboration of the earthquake also points to a central aspect of his entire thought, namely what Eric Voegelin identified as the clearly Gnostic elements in modern philosophy: the tendency to ground and organize thought and cognition in a heroic struggle against the chaos of the surrounding world. In this Gnostic enterprise, as we shall see in Chapter 5, he had been anticipated by Descartes and Hobbes. Kant’s reaction, and his whole philosophy, therefore exemplifies within the realm of thought what Weber identified as the inner-weltliche world-mastery as a path of salvation characteristic of the Judaeo-Christian tradition as it developed in the West. Liminality and its Complementary Concepts The analytical potential of liminality needs to be enhanced by other anthropological concepts, which to a surprising degree have been ignored by social theorists (Szakolczai 2009: 153; Horvath and Thomassen 2008). Three such concepts can be singled out: imitation, schismogenesis and trickster (logics). These complementary concepts become particularly relevant once we move into liminal situations without clearly defined boundaries of space and time, and without pre-defined masters of ceremony. They become necessary especially once we engage liminality in ‘mass’ societies, where ‘crowds’ or ‘publics’ constitute Turner’s communitas. Imitation, Repetition, Contagion: Plato, Kierkegaard, Tarde and Girard As is well-known, mimesis, the imitative aspect of human behaviour, was a central concern for Plato.
